EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex and potent winter storm crossed western and central Wyoming and brought heavy snow and high wind to many locations. Strong winds occurred ahead of the storm in portions of central Wyoming, with some of the strongest gusts were 78 mph at Fales Rock and 75 mph along Wyoming Boulevard on the south side of Casper. Following that, heavy snow fell. Over a foot of new snow fell in portions of the Tetons, Absarokas and Casper Mountain as well as in Yellowstone Park. In the lower elevations, the heaviest snow fell around Casper where up to 15 inches was measured. Other areas with over 6 inches included around Rock Springs, northern Big Horn County and around Bondurant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of Yellowstone Park. The heaviest amount was 14 inches at the Canyon SNOTEL site and at the Old Faithful Ranger Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a strong pressure gradient and strong low level winds mixing to the surface brought high winds to wind prone areas of northern and central Wyoming. The strongest winds occurred in the vicinity of Clark where gusts as high as 93 mph were recorded. In central Wyoming, some of the highest wind gusts included 83 mph at the Fales Rock RAWS site in the Green Mountains and 68 mph along Wyoming Boulevard south of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred in portions of the Cody Foothills. The strongest wind gust recorded was 93 mph 5 miles west-northwest of Clark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid level winds mixed to the surface ahead of an advancing cold front and brought high winds to the wind prone areas across central Wyoming. The strongest winds were near Clark where winds gusted as high as 96 mph. Elsewhere, winds gusted to 81 mph along Wyoming Boulevard south of Casper and a gust of 79 mph was recorded at Fales Rock in the Green Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred in portions of the Cody Foothills. The strongest winds were in the vicinity of Clark where winds gusted as high as 96 mph. High winds even made it into the west side of Cody where a gust of 58 mph was recorded.

Arctic air at the surface, combined with a moist flow off the Pacific, resulted in an over-running event which brought heavy snow across the entire Billings Forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 3 inches along with severe driving conditions were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front, accompanied by snow, moved across the Billings Forecast Area during the afternoon and evening of the 30th. In addition, Pacific moisture moved overhead across the arctic air mass during the evening of the 30th and into the early morning hours of the 31st. This resulted in heavy snow across most of the Billings Forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches were reported across the foothills. In addition, Interstate 90 was closed from the Montana/Wyoming border south to Interstate 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced strong wind gusts to 60 mph across portions of southeast Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds affected the wind-prone areas as well as the Laramie Valley and southeast Wyoming plains.  Frequent wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor along Interstate 80 near Buford measured the reported wind gust. A nearby Union Pacific Railroad sensor had a max gust during this period of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew across many of the wind prone areas of southeastern Wyoming in addition to the mountain valleys and foothills along the high plains. Winds generally were gusting into the mid 60 mph range though some sites reached into the middle 70 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Site four miles west of Muddy Gap measured strong sustained winds over 40 mph with gusts exceeding 58 mph through the period.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL LARAMIE RANGE AND SOUTHWEST PLATTE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew across portions of southeastern Wyoming with the winds coming off the Laramie Range near Vedauwoo and Bordeaux. Nearby Wyoming Department of Transportation sensors measured sustained winds of over 40 mph and gusts in the upper 50s and even to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sensor near Bordeaux measured in addition to strong gusts a sustained wind measuring 44 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sensor along Interstate 80 by Vedauwoo measured sustained wind of 43 mph, gusting to 56 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the region producing packed gradient flow across the wind prone areas of southeastern Wyoming. Moisture associated with the system produced snowfall across the area especially the higher terrains of the Snowy and Sierra Madre Ranges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Medicine Bow site measured snow water equivalent of 2.1 inches and applying a twelve-to-one snow to liquid ratio brought estimated total snowfall value of 25.2 inches for a twenty hour accumulation period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Whitaker measured wind gusts of 58 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 66 mph at 17/2330 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tightening pressure gradient ahead of an advancing cold front increased southwest flow across portions of central Wyoming and brought high winds to some locations. The highest winds were along Wyoming Boulevard south of Casper where winds gusted as high as 74 mph. Strong winds also occurred at the RAWS sites in the Green Mountains with wind gusts to 67 and 63 mph at Fales Rock and Camp Creek, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT weather sensor along Wyoming Boulevard south of Casper saw numerous wind gusts over 58 mph, including a maximum of 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances and a supply of Pacific moisture brought locally heavy snow to portions of western Wyoming. In the mountains, they highest amounts fell in the Salt and Wyoming Range, including a maximum of 21 inches at the Willow Creek SNOTEL site. In the Tetons, the Jackson Hole Ski Resort had 16 inches of new snow. In the Jackson Valley, amounts generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches. The Star Valley also saw heavy snow, with amounts ranging from 5 inches at Afton to 12 inches at Alpine. Strong southwest wind also occurred with the system, with the Camp Creek RAWS in the Green Mountains experiencing a maximum wind gust of 84 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts through the Jackson Valley generally ranged from 5 to 13 inches, including 8 inches in the town of Jackson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific low pressure system tracked east across Wyoming and Colorado and produced widespread moderate to heavy snow.  Strong winds of 35 to 55 mph created significant blowing and drifting snow and low visibility throughout much of southeast Wyoming.  Portions of Interstates 25 and 80 were closed due to dangerous travel conditions.  Total snowfall ranged from 6 to 16 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow was observed at Hanna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching Wyoming, tightened the pressure gradient across the state. In addition, strong mid level winds mixed to the surface and brought very strong winds to portions of Wyoming. The strongest winds occurred in the Lee of the Wind River Mountains. In the Green and Rattlesnake Range, Camp Creek had multiple gusts over 75 mph with a maximum gust of 101 mph. Wind gusts to 82 mph were recorded at Red Canyon and Beaver Rim. At the Beaver Rim overlook, a tractor trailer truck was overturned by the high winds. Other notable wind gusts included 86 mph along the North Folk of the Popo Agie outside of Lander and 80 mph at Poker Creek in the Big Horn Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred in many portions of the Cody foothills. The strongest winds were near Clark, where a 97 mph wind gust occurred 5 miles WNW of Clark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances and a supply of Pacific moisture brought locally heavy snow to portions of western Wyoming. In the mountains, they highest amounts fell in the Salt and Wyoming Range, including a maximum of 21 inches at the Willow Creek SNOTEL site. In the Tetons, the Jackson Hole Ski Resort had 16 inches of new snow. In the Jackson Valley, amounts generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches. The Star Valley also saw heavy snow, with amounts ranging from 5 inches at Afton to 12 inches at Alpine. Strong southwest wind also occurred with the system, with the Camp Creek RAWS in the Green Mountains experiencing a maximum wind gust of 84 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell through much of the Tetons and Gros Venture mountains. The highest amount measured was 16 inches at the top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought heavy snow to much of northeastern Wyoming during the overnight and morning hours. Rain changed to snow during the evening and snow became heavy at times during the overnight and early morning hours. Snowfall totals of four to eight inches were common across much of the area, with a band of heavier snowfall across northern Campbell and Crook Counties producing around a foot of snow.

A pair of cold fronts moved across Wyoming and brought areas of heavy snow across northern and western portions of the state. The Big Horn Mountains saw heavy snow with some notable amounts including 20 inches at the Powder River SNOTEL and 16 inches at Shell Creek. Some of the other high amounts in the mountains included 19 inches at Evening Star in the Absarokas, 16 inches at Blind Bull Summit in the Salt and Wyoming Range and 13 inches at Togwotee Pass in the Tetons. In the Big Horn Basin, 10 inches of snow fell at Ten Sleep and anywhere from 6 to 9 inches in the Greybull area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex and potent winter storm crossed western and central Wyoming and brought heavy snow and high wind to many locations. Strong winds occurred ahead of the storm in portions of central Wyoming, with some of the strongest gusts were 78 mph at Fales Rock and 75 mph along Wyoming Boulevard on the south side of Casper. Following that, heavy snow fell. Over a foot of new snow fell in portions of the Tetons, Absarokas and Casper Mountain as well as in Yellowstone Park. In the lower elevations, the heaviest snow fell around Casper where up to 15 inches was measured. Other areas with over 6 inches included around Rock Springs, northern Big Horn County and around Bondurant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Star Valley. The highest amount recorded was 8 inches to the NNE of Thayne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbance brought very heavy snow to localized areas of western Wyoming. Across portions of the Tetons, persistent convective banding set up and brought periods of 2 to 3 inch an hour snowfall. At the Grand Targhee Ski Resort, 57 inches of snow was recorded through the storm. Jackson Hole received 29 inches of new snow. Heavy snow fell in the Jackson Valley as well with 8 to 14 inches around the valley. Lincoln County also saw heavy snow with over 1 foot common in the Star Valley with a maximum of 19 inches near Thayne.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of Yellowstone Park. Some of the higher amounts included 15 inches at Two Oceans Plateau and 14 inches at Lewis Lake Divide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low over Idaho combined with a surface low over central Wyoming brought a late season heavy snow event to the Sheridan Foothills area. Tight surface pressure gradients associated with the surface low resulted in gusty northwest winds and considerable blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 mph with significant blowing and drifting snow were reported. I-90 was closed south of Sheridan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving into northern Wyoming combined with potent jet dynamics to produce heavy snow across portions of northern Wyoming. Over a foot of snow fell in portions of Yellowstone Park, the Tetons and the Absarokas. Heavy snow fell across the lower elevations as well. Up to 8 inches fell around the Cody area and combined with strong winds to produce near zero visibility and road closures. Strong winds and heavy snow also combined to close roads in northern Johnson County. Further south, high winds were the problem in portions of Fremont and Sweetwater Counties. Winds gusted to 71 mph at Fales Rock RAWS site and 63 mph at Bitter Creek along Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of Yellowstone. The highest amount was 13 inches at Sylvan Lake.

Snow totals in the mountains of the Sierra Madre and Snowy Ranges in the 20 to even up to 60 inches of new snowfall. Areas in the High Plains received half a foot of snow to a foot and half in some places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rains from thunderstorms and a clogged culvert allowed water to flow across Highway 189 around 6 miles north of Labarge. After the clog was removed, the water receded and the highway reopened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of thunderstorms moved across central Fremont County and produced hail in different locations. Off duty National Weather Service employees reported up to nickel size hail around Riverton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty National Weather Service employee reported nickel size hail at the intersection of US 26 and Missouri Valley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal boundary was again the focus for severe thunderstorms for a third consecutive day. Natrona County was the focus once again. Large hail was reported a couple of locations. The first was in Midwest, where one piece of a splitting supercell thunderstorm produced quarter size hail. Another storm dropped golf ball sized hail to the southwest of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Midwest Police Chief reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low and potent jet dynamics moved across Wyoming and brought severe weather to portions of central Wyoming. The strongest thunderstorms moved across eastern Washakie and western Johnson counties. Two tornadoes touched down and downed many trees along the Washakie / Johnson County border. The storms also produced large hail up to the size of ping pong balls. Another strong thunderstorm produced nickel size hail at Winchester.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported nickel size hail in Winchester.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CO-OP Observer near Big Trails reported half dollar to ping pong ball size hail 3 miles north of Big Trails.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were measured and felt across much of southeastern Wyoming. Several locations across the wind prone areas and high plains had gusts into the middle 60 to middle 70 mph range. Heavy snow followed closely behind the high winds with reports of heavy snow for much of the western mountains and northern high plains. Snow totals in the mountains of the Sierra Madre and Snowy Ranges in the 20 to even up to 60 inches of new snowfall. Areas in the High Plains received half a foot of snow to a foot and half in some places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sensor along Interstate 80 seven miles northeast of Buford measured the strong wind gust while other sensors along the interstate had gusts in the upper 50 and 60 mph range.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado with maximum winds at 130 mph touched down in Northeast Washakie county in a heavily forested area at around 8000 feet. The tornado downed and snapped off around 1,000 trees near ground level during its short path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Wyoming and brought showers and thunderstorms. The strongest thunderstorm occurred in northeast Natrona County where quarter size hail fell Midwest. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported nickel to quarter size hail in Midwest and Edgerton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell thunderstorm tracked eastward across northern Campbell and northern Crook Counties. The storm produced large hail and gusty winds before moving into northwestern South Dakota.

A supercell thunderstorm tracked east-northeast across central Campbell County. As the storm approached Gillette, it produced multiple tornadoes. A storm survey, along with photos and video, determined that four tornadoes occurred just west and north of Gillette. The largest tornado was on the ground for about ten minutes and briefly reached EF-3 strength as it tracked about ten miles west-northwest of Gillette. There were two injuries, with significant damage to numerous buildings and homes along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down about ten miles west-northwest of downtown Gillette. It was on the ground for about 11 minutes over the Oriva Hills subdivision. The tornado destroyed and damaged numerous homes and outbuildings, rolled vehicles, and snapped electrical transmission poles and trees. Top wind speeds were estimated at 136 mph, so the tornado was rated as an EF-3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of July 5th. In northern Johnson County. wind gusts of 58 and 60 mph were recorded. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were measured and felt across much of southeastern Wyoming. Several locations across the wind prone areas and high plains had gusts into the middle 60 to middle 70 mph range. Heavy snow followed closely behind the high winds with reports of heavy snow for much of the western mountains and northern high plains. Snow totals in the mountains of the Sierra Madre and Snowy Ranges in the 20 to even up to 60 inches of new snowfall. Areas in the High Plains received half a foot of snow to a foot and half in some places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sensor located seven miles south-southwest of Meriden or nearby Gun Barrel measured the reported wind gust. A nearby site also measured a sustained wind of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stationary front over the area, a shortwave moving across the area and strong jet dynamics brought rounds of severe thunderstorms over northern and central Wyoming. The hardest hit town this day was Lovell where a combination of 70 mph winds and golf ball size hail produced widespread damage with hundreds of broken windows and many houses with damaged siding. Strong winds also downed many trees and limbs. The same storm produced 79 mph winds at the Greybull airport. Elsewhere, severe thunderstorms moved through Johnson and Natrona Counties. There were numerous reports of quarter to golf ball size hail from west of Buffalo through Midwest and into Casper. A weak landspout tornado also touched down north of Casper. Another storm produced quarter size hail in Thermopolis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed over the Bighorn Mountains and became severe as it moved south-southeast across Johnson County. There were multiple reports of quarter to golf ball size hail along the path by spotters and on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning strikes ignited a wildfire in grassland northeast of Worland on the evening of July 8th, mainly north of US Highway 16 and Wyoming Highway 31 in Washakie and Big Horn Counties. The fire grew rapidly on July 8th and 9thl causing the closure of Highway 31 for a time. The fire was largely contained by July 13th. A total of around 43000 acres was burned. A majority of the fire was on public land.

The combination of a stationary front over the area, a shortwave moving across the area and strong jet dynamics brought rounds of severe thunderstorms over northern and central Wyoming. The hardest hit town this day was Lovell where a combination of 70 mph winds and golf ball size hail produced widespread damage with hundreds of broken windows and many houses with damaged siding. Strong winds also downed many trees and limbs. The same storm produced 79 mph winds at the Greybull airport. Elsewhere, severe thunderstorms moved through Johnson and Natrona Counties. There were numerous reports of quarter to golf ball size hail from west of Buffalo through Midwest and into Casper. A weak landspout tornado also touched down north of Casper. Another storm produced quarter size hail in Thermopolis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported hail ranging from quarter to ping pong ball size from Bar Nunn into the east side of Casper. Some of larger hail damaged a carport in Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stationary front over the area, a shortwave moving across the area and strong jet dynamics brought rounds of severe thunderstorms over northern and central Wyoming. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm brought high winds through much of northern Big Horn County. The strongest wind gust recorded was 79 mph at the Greybull Airport. Other notable gusts included 62 mph at Cowley and 59 mph at Frannie and Lovell. The strong wind combined with golf ball size hail to produce widespread damage across the city of Lovell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent stationary front over central Wyoming brought a second day of severe weather. Johnson County was the hardest hit as two supercell thunderstorms brought large hail to many areas, including the city of Buffalo. Numerous windows were broken and siding damaged with a combination of strong winds and golf ball size hail. Crop damage was also widespread across much of Johnson County. Elsewhere, up to half dollar size hail fell around the Casper area and up to lime size to the southeast of Casper. Half dollar size hail also fell in Bighorn County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported penny size hail in Midwest.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was measured 29 miles north of Torrington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent stationary front over central Wyoming brought a second day of severe weather. Johnson County was the hardest hit as two supercell thunderstorms brought large hail to many areas, including the city of Buffalo. Numerous windows were broken and siding damaged with a combination of strong winds and golf ball size hail. Crop damage was also widespread across much of Johnson County. Elsewhere, up to half dollar size hail fell around the Casper area and up to lime size to the southeast of Casper. Half dollar size hail also fell in Bighorn County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Buffalo airport reported a wind gust to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first winter storm impacted portions of Sheridan County. Heavy snow was reported along the foothills of the Big Horn Mountains. Heavy, wet snow blanketed trees with full foliage which resulted in power outages from Dayton to Story.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5 to 6 inches were reported in Story. In addition, power outages occurred from Dayton to Story.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of rain and snow melt brought minor flooding around DuBois. Flooding was restricted to the low lands and caused no damage. Flooding near Highway 132 also caused the closing of a lane of traffic along the Blue Sky Highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Wind River near Dubois. No damage occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fremont County emergency management reported flooding near the Wind River and Highway 132. The south lane of traffic was covered with water for a day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed in the lee of the Wind River Mountains and became severe as it moved into Lander. Quarter size hail was reported in the city of Lander.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter size hail in Lander.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northward into Wyoming with copious amounts of moisture. This spawned thunderstorms that brought heavy rain to many areas. Along Highway 120 to the south of Meeteetse, the fire department reported a foot of water across the road for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A foot of water was reported to be flowing across Highway 120 around 5 miles south of Meeteetse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of heavy rain, snow melt and a clogged culvert resulted in flooding near the Highway 16 bridge along Ten Sleep Creek. Several campgrounds were flooded. The water got close the top of the bridge but never topped it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washakie County sheriff deputies reported flooding around the Ten Sleep Creek bridge along Highway 16. The water flooded several campsites. The water also got close to surface of the bridge but never topped it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of rain and snow melt produced minor flooding around Medicine Lodge Creek as the creek rose to flood stage. Flooding remained in low lying areas and caused no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Medicine Lodge Creek rose to flood stage briefly on the morning of May 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain brought flooding in portions of Johnson County north of Buffalo. Highway 193 near Fort Phil Kearney was closed due to flooding from Rock Creek. Water was also crossing Old Highway 87. No structural damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flooding near Fort Phil Kearney that covered old Highway 87 and temporarily closed Highway 193.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed over portions of central and southern Wyoming. In Sweetwater County, a tornado formed near Granger. The tornado was photographed by many people. The tornado remained over open country and caused no damage. In addition, flash flooding was reported over rural portions of Sweetwater County. In Fremont County, another tornado briefly touched down southeast of South Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado near Granger was well photographed by the general public and reported on local media. The tornado remained over open country and caused no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper level low moving across Wyoming and large amounts of moisture produced a severe weather outbreak across portions of central Wyoming. Natrona County, including Casper was hard hit with numerous reports of large hail ranging in size from quarter to golf ball size. Two funnel clouds were also sited. Across the Big Horn Basin, downburst winds were the main threat. Wind gusts of over 70 mph were recorded in portions of Park and Big Horn Counties. Along a line from Manderson to Basin to Greybull in Big Horn County, a storm survey counted 33 downed trees and 13 power lines caused by the high winds. Trees also fell on several houses in Basin and Greybull and a roof was blown off a business in Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and off duty NWS meteorologists reported from penny to golf ball size hail west of Alcova.

An upper level disturbance passing across Wyoming brought a couple of severe thunderstorms to the state. The first episode was near Thermopolis, where a funnel cloud was sighted by several spotters and lowered about three quarters of the way to the ground. Another thunderstorm formed in the Lee of the Absarokas and produced quarter size hail to the east of Cody. Some rotation was also observed with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained spotters reported a funnel cloud to the east-southeast of Thermopolis over the Owl Creek Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed in the lee of the Big Horn Mountains and strengthened rapidly as it encountered higher dew point air. It became severe east of Interstate 25. A trained spotter to the east of Kaycee reported half dollar size hail as the storm passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported half dollar size hail 7 miles east of Kaycee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved into Wyoming from the south and was accompanied by copious amounts of moisture. With cool air in place over the area, heavy snow fell in some of the mountain ranges above 8500 feet. Some of the highest amounts included 16 inches at Marquette in the Absarokas and 14 inches at Hobbs Park in the Wind River Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Absarokas. The highest amount was 16 inches at the Marquette SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some mid and high level monsoonal moisture moving in from the south and weak shortwave approaching the area triggered some high based convection. With a very large dew point depression, some of the showers and thundertorms produced very strong winds as the thunderstorms collapsed. At the Lander Airport, a collapsing high based thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 63 mph. Another collapsing thunderstorm produced a 59 mph wind gust at the Worland airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Lander Airport reported a wind gust of 63 mph from a collapsing thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Roosevelt Fire began as a campfire that was left unattended and spread to eventually burned over 61,000 acres in Lincoln and Sublette Counties. The fire was reported by a father and son hunting party on September 15th in the Wyoming Range. They attempted to escape but were forced to jump into a creek. They suffered burns and had to be airlifted to a burn unit in Salt Lake City. Fueled by warm temperatures, gusty to strong winds and low humidity, the fire than progressed eastward and prompted evacuations of residential areas northwest of Pinedale. The worst damage was at the Hoback Ranch subdivision where 55 of the 150 homes were destroyed. The fire also prompted the closure of US 189 / 191 three times due to firefighting efforts. At the fire's peak, 1175 personal were on scene. One fire fighter also injuries while fighting the fire. The total cost of fighting the fire was over $23 million dollars. The fire continued into October. Please see October for the conclusion of this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across Wyoming and brought severe weather to northern portions of the state. There was a decent amount of shear with the system. As a result several funnel clouds were spotted. No tornadoes touched down however. Large hail also fell in many locations, including a maximum size of 1.50 inches near Ten Sleep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell along the path of a severe thunderstorm. The maximum hail size reported was quarter size 7 miles east of Pitchfork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across Wyoming and brought strong thunderstorms to portions of central Wyoming. One thunderstorm formed in the lee of the Absarokas and developed rotation. A funnel cloud developed over northwestern Washakie County and was photographed by few people. The storm then continued northeastward into Johnson County and dropped penny size hail east of Buffalo. The worst damage was in eastern Big Horn County where a microburst occurred near the Paint Rock Lakes Area. Hundreds of trees were downed and forest roads and trails were closed. Winds were estimated to be anywhere from 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported a funnel cloud northwest of Worland.

The worst damage was in eastern Big Horn County where a microburst occurred near the Paint Rock Lakes Area. Hundreds of trees were downed and forest roads and trails were closed. Winds were estimated to be anywhere from 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny size hail along Interstate 90 to the southeast of Buffalo.

The storm then continued northeastward into Johnson County and dropped penny size hail east of Buffalo. The worst damage was in eastern Big Horn County where a microburst occurred near the Paint Rock Lakes Area. Hundreds of trees were downed and forest roads and trails were closed. Winds were estimated to be anywhere from 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a microburst that downed several hundred trees in the Bighorn National Forest. Wind gusts were estimated to be between 80 and 90 mph.

As a result several funnel clouds were spotted. No tornadoes touched down however. Large hail also fell in many locations, including a maximum size of 1.50 inches near Ten Sleep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a funnel cloud to the west of Meeteetse.

Showers and thunderstorms developed in western Wyoming ahead of a cold front. SOme storms began to rotate as well. In Sublette County, a funnel cloud was spotted. The funnel was photographed by several people. The funnel then dissipated before reaching the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sublette County Emergency Manager reported a funnel cloud near the Big Piney airport. The funnel cloud reached about two thirds of the way to the ground but never touched down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northern portions of Wyoming on June 21st. One storm formed in the lee of the Absarokas and rapidly strengthened as it approached Highway 120. A funnel cloud was spotted northwest of Thermopolis. The same storm produced penny size hail to the south-southeast of Meeteetse. Another storm brought strong winds to Johnson County, where wind gusts of 59 and 61 mph where measured at Buffalo and Linch respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported a funnel cloud northwest of Thermopolis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three slowly moving thunderstorms merged over Midwest and brought large amounts of hail and street flooding. A rain gauge measured over 2.5 inches of precipitation with rain and melting hail. The hail flowed into a wash and drifted up to four feet in some areas. Flooding was also noted in Salt Creek Oil Field as well as in portions of Midwest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter size fell in Midwest. There was a large amount of small hail as well that caused localized flooding. The hail flowed into a wash and piled up several feet in places.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large amounts of hail up to nickel size fell in northern Fremont County, especially along Missouri Valley Road. The hail covered the ground to the depth of a few inches in places, including in Midvale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boat capsized around 3.5 miles southeast of the Buckboard Marina on Flaming Gorge reservoir around 1 pm after gusty winds developed and the water became choppy. The boat was swamped and eventually capsized. A 48 year old man drowned as a result. He was the only one not wearing a life vest. Two other men suffered minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm tracked east across central Campbell and southern Crook Counties, producing large hail and strong wind gusts. The storm became tornadic as it moved east-southeast of Sundance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed southeast of Sundance and tracked generally to the east into South Dakota. The majority of damage occurred in wooded areas, with widespread snapped trees observed throughout the tornado path. Some structural damage was also observed southeast of Sundance just east of highway 585, near the beginning of the tornado's path. Based on the damage, the tornado was rated at an EF-1, with peak wind speeds estimated at 105 mph.

A supercell thunderstorm tracked east-northeast across central Campbell County. As the storm approached Gillette, it produced multiple tornadoes. A storm survey, along with photos and video, determined that four tornadoes occurred just west and north of Gillette. The largest tornado was on the ground for about ten minutes and briefly reached EF-3 strength as it tracked about ten miles west-northwest of Gillette. There were two injuries, with significant damage to numerous buildings and homes along its path.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down about ten miles west of downtown Gillette between Montgomery Road and Echeta Road, south of the Oriva Hills subdivision. It was on the ground for about four minutes. Since it left no visible damage, the tornado was rated as EF-0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm tracked east-northeast across central Campbell County. As the storm approached Gillette, it produced multiple tornadoes. A storm survey, along with photos and video, determined that four tornadoes occurred just west and north of Gillette. The largest tornado was on the ground for about ten minutes and briefly reached EF-3 strength as it tracked about ten miles west-northwest of Gillette. There were two injuries, with significant damage to numerous buildings and homes along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short-lived landspout tornado developed three miles north-northeast of downtown Gillette in the Warrior Hills subdivision. It damaged a large shed, flipped a camper onto a vehicle, destroyed another camper, and damaged several wooded fences. Winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph, giving the tornado a rating as a weak EF-1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm tracked east-northeast across central Campbell County. As the storm approached Gillette, it produced multiple tornadoes. A storm survey, along with photos and video, determined that four tornadoes occurred just west and north of Gillette. The largest tornado was on the ground for about ten minutes and briefly reached EF-3 strength as it tracked about ten miles west-northwest of Gillette. There were two injuries, with significant damage to numerous buildings and homes along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down over the west pit of the Eagle Butte Mine, about seven miles north of downtown Gillette. The tornado traveled east-northeast for about eight minutes before dissipating just east of the Dry Fork Power Plant. The tornado snapped and bent over seven power poles, damaged the control room building at the mine, and damaged several vehicles at the Eagle Butte Mine and Dry Fork Power Plant. With wind speeds estimated at 100 mph, the tornado was rated as an EF-1.

A Pacific system crossed Wyoming and brought heavy snow to portions of western Wyoming and high wind to central Wyoming. Over a foot of new snow fell at the Jackson Hole Ski resort with up to 10 inches reported around Alpine. Meanwhile, East of the Divide high winds were the story. The highest wind gusts were the RAWS sites in the Green Mountains where gusts of 73 mph were recorded. In the lower elevations, the highest wind gust recorded was 71 mph along Wyoming Boulevard to the south of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor along Wyoming Boulevard recorded many wind gusts past 58 mph, including a maximum gust of 71 mph.

Strong winds were measured and felt across much of southeastern Wyoming. Several locations across the wind prone areas and high plains had gusts into the middle 60 to middle 70 mph range. Heavy snow followed closely behind the high winds with reports of heavy snow for much of the western mountains and northern high plains. Snow totals in the mountains of the Sierra Madre and Snowy Ranges in the 20 to even up to 60 inches of new snowfall. Areas in the High Plains received half a foot of snow to a foot and half in some places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold arctic air with breezy conditions made ushering in the New Year a bit chilly for southeastern Wyoming. In Cheyenne, the annual New Year's Ball Drop was cancelled due to the cold temperatures and wind chills. Coldest wind chill measured was minus twenty-three with even colder wind chills felt the morning of New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mayor of Cheyenne after discussion with the Laramie County Emergency Manager and a briefing by the National Weather Service Cheyenne, Wyoming Office made the executive decision to cancel the annual New Year's Eve Ball Drop due to cold temperatures and dangerous wind chills. A Union Pacific Railroad weather sensor had wind chills the morning of New Year's Eve in the minus 20s with the Cheyenne Airport ASOS reporting a wind chill of -25 later on.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific weather system moved across western and central Wyoming and brought locally heavy snow and high winds. In the western mountains, the heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where 20 inches of new snow fell at Grand Targhee. The heaviest snow in the lower elevations occurred at Alpine where 10 inches of snow fell. In areas East of the Divide, the heaviest snow fell near Thermopolis where up to a foot of snow fell. Elsewhere, snowfall was lighter. However, in some areas snow combined with wind gusts over 50 mph the bring near zero visibility. The caused the closure of roads in Johnson County for several hours. Other roads were closed in portions of Natrona, Hot Springs and Fremont Counties. Strong winds also occurred ahead of the system in the Cody Foothills, where a wind gust of 81 mph occurred the south of Clark. The winds extended into Cody itself where the Cody airport had a wind gust to 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 81 mph occurred to the south of Clark. At the Cody airport, there was a maximum wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds mainly across the wind prone areas of southeast Wyoming were howling with intensity through the 11th and 12th. Several Wyoming Department of Transportation sensors along both Interstate 80 and Interstate 25 had measured gusts into the upper 50s and low 60 mph range. A vehicular accident across the Laramie Valley just west of the city of Laramie resulted in the fatality of a male individual when his vehicle slid off a slick patch of road caused by blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A single vehicle car crash occurred at milepost 302 on the eastbound lanes of Interstate 80 about eight miles west of Laramie. The driver lost control of his vehicle to which the vehicle then left the roadway and overturned. The driver succumbed to his injuries at the scene. The interstate was reportedly icy likely from  strong winds blowing snow onto the roadways and refreezing. In addition, the driver of vehicle was reported as not wearing a seat belt. A nearby Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor near Herrick Lane had wind gusts measuring around 30 mph around the time of the crash with max winds during the event at 50 mph at 3:00 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 31 year old man riding in the Wyoming Range to the East of Alpine was riding a snowmobile in steep terrain when he triggered a 100 foot by 100 foot slab avalanche. The avalanche flipped his snowmobile and he was pinned under the snowmobile for an hour before being dug out. He was pronounced dead at the scene.

A 31 year old man was back country skiing just south of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort, in an area called the Spacewalk Couloir. The man triggered an avalanche and he was tumbled the full length of the feature. The man was not buried but suffered leg and hip injuries. He was rescued and brought a hospital in Jackson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds and a couple tornadoes over portions  of Converse and Platte counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first observed evidence of the tornado was near Rattlesnake Hill just north of Wagonhound Road.  The tornado moved to the south and quickly grew stronger.  The NWS survey team continued to investigate some video evidence that the tornado may have been on the ground prior to Rattlesnake Hill.  The tornado snapped several trees and power poles along Wagonhound Road, and blew several large rolls of hay nearly 300 yards from a large hay bale stack.   The tornado intensified as it moved south toward a ranch, reaching EF3 intensity through the barn and residence, destroying several buildings and causing significant damage to the ranch house.  The ranch house had large sections of roof structure removed, and a quonset hut tractor storage area just west of the house was destroyed.  Two large utility trailers weighing several thousand pounds were lofted and had not been located, with the exception of one axel set.  Large cottonwood trees were stripped of all vegetation with just the largest limbs near the center of the trees remaining intact.  The tornado continued south-southeast before lifting in the vicinity of Poison Lake.  Start and end and locations and times of the tornado were estimated from eyewitness accounts and radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms generated large hail over parts of Niobrara and Goshen counties in addition to a well photogenic tornadic supercell over Albany County. Two tornadoes, the parent and a satellite, were confirmed by photos from storm chasers and official NWS Storm Survey. The parent tornado was rated as an EF-3 while the satellite was rated as an EF-2. Some residential damage was caused by the satellite tornado including the lifting off the foundation of a attached garage. The parent EF-3 tornado bent galvanized steel utility poles 90 degrees at base.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed six miles north of Torrington.

Thunderstorms generated large hail over parts of Niobrara and Goshen counties in addition to a well photogenic tornadic supercell over Albany County. Two tornadoes, the parent and a satellite, were confirmed by photos from storm chasers and official NWS Storm Survey. The parent tornado was rated as an EF-3 while the satellite was rated as an EF-2. Some residential damage was caused by the satellite tornado including the lifting off the foundation of a attached garage. The parent EF-3 tornado bent galvanized steel utility poles 90 degrees at base.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball size hail was observed south of Lusk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms generated large hail over parts of Niobrara and Goshen counties in addition to a well photogenic tornadic supercell over Albany County. Two tornadoes, the parent and a satellite, were confirmed by photos from storm chasers and official NWS Storm Survey. The parent tornado was rated as an EF-3 while the satellite was rated as an EF-2. Some residential damage was caused by the satellite tornado including the lifting off the foundation of a attached garage. The parent EF-3 tornado bent galvanized steel utility poles 90 degrees at base.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell thunderstorm developed a tornado three to four miles west of Highway 30. The tornado continued an eastward track and intensified rapidly over the mainly open fields. Numerous wooden power poles were snapped along County Road 121, along with several galvanized steel utility poles, which were bent 90 degrees at the base. Grass was scoured out of the ground in a wide swath approximately one third of a mile in width near the intersection of County Road 121 and Cattle drive. The observed tornado continued east into the Laramie Range near King Mountain Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms generated large hail over parts of Niobrara and Goshen counties in addition to a well photogenic tornadic supercell over Albany County. Two tornadoes, the parent and a satellite, were confirmed by photos from storm chasers and official NWS Storm Survey. The parent tornado was rated as an EF-3 while the satellite was rated as an EF-2. Some residential damage was caused by the satellite tornado including the lifting off the foundation of a attached garage. The parent EF-3 tornado bent galvanized steel utility poles 90 degrees at base.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A satellite tornado formed two miles to the south of the parent tornado. The tornado damaged treetops and caused significant structural damage to a well-built attached garage, which collapsed as it lifted off the foundation and shifted to the east. In addition, several nearby structures had minor damage to siding and shingles as the tornado was observed moving through the Antelope Ridge Loop subdivision, six miles north of Laramie, and into open country.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds and a couple tornadoes over portions  of Converse and Platte counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second tornado formed as the first tornado was lifting to the west, touching down near the West Fork of La Bonte Creek and Esterbrook Road.  The tornado quickly strengthened to EF1 intensity as it moved along La Bonte Creek passing near a ranch house off Esterbrook Road.  Most of the damage was to trees that were snapped mid trunk in a 150 yard path.  The tornado lifted as it moved east of La Bonte Creek.  The NWS survey team lost track of the tornado as it  moved southeast into open country, so it is possible that track extended farther than the survey results indicated.  The survey team also continued to investigate reports of damage at Esterbrook Campground to the southeast, to see if this damage was associated with the same tornado.  Start and end times and locations and times of the tornado were estimated from eyewitness accounts and radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms and supercells formed across the high plains of southeastern Wyoming producing large hail and two confirmed tornadoes. The first tornado was rated as an EF-2 as it produced damage to residences and vehicles in Federal. A second tornado formed near the Whitaker exit along Interstate 25 though the damage done to a car but there was not a damage indicator to assign, so it was rated as EF-Unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant tornado damage was noted near Federal, Wyoming where eight structures were impacted along Mountain Shadow Drive and Mountain Shadow Lane. The tornado initially touched down just northeast of Federal, tracking south, before lifting south of Federal. Significant damage consistent with a EF-2 tornado was observed to a manufactured home just east of Federal, where the roof had been removed, garage destroyed, while exterior walls were left attached to the foundation and intact. The tornado continued south tossing numerous livestock and camper trailers. High-end EF-2 damage was observed to a single family residence farther south, where the roof and a few exterior walls were removed. A garage and exterior shed was also destroyed at the residence. The tornado continued south removing the roof of another home half a miles southeast of Federal. Numerous damage to roofs around the area was also noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept across western and central Wyoming from Friday into Saturday and brought heavy snow and difficult travel conditions to many areas. The heaviest snow fell in Absarokas where 21 inches fell in Pahaska. A foot or more of new snow also fell in portions of Yellowstone Park and the Tetons. The heaviest snow in the lower elevations fell in northern Wyoming. Some notable amounts include 10 inches at Bondurant, 9 inches in Cody and 10 inches in Thermopolis. Even in areas that received less snow, the combination of snow, gusty wind and black ice from rapidly falling temperatures brought very difficult travel conditions. Interstates 25 and 80 were shut down for a time, as well South Pass over the Wind River Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of Yellowstone Park. The SNOTEL sites at Lewis Lake Divide, Parker Peak and Two Ocean Plateau recorded 12 inches of new snow.
